narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. Your profile includes a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which is above the typical student's average, an LSAT score of 31, middle-family income, full-time student status, and school deciles of 4th in both your first and third years—meaning you ranked in the middle of your class. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 48% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is high enough to trigger a rejection as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction wasn't random; it weighed your specific background against patterns seen in past students. The two biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your LSAT score, which is below the average of around 37 and signaled higher risk to the model, and your middle-family income bracket, which unfortunately aligned with trends of tougher outcomes for students in similar situations. On a brighter note, your stronger-than-average undergraduate GPA was the most influential positive factor, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass, while your full-time status and first-year decile also helped a bit by matching successful profiles. Your third-year decile had a smaller nudge toward risk, but overall, the LSAT and income tipped the scales just enough compared to peers with higher test scores from varied backgrounds. Many admitted students have LSATs closer to or above average, even with similar GPAs, highlighting how that one metric often stands out in these decisions. This doesn't define your potential—it's just one model's snapshot based on historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, gathering stronger letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as a Black woman in academia, or applying to programs with holistic reviews that value your unique story. You're capable of great things; use this as fuel to strengthen your application and find the right fit. Keep pushing—you've got this.
